policy.title: RBI Issues Final Guidelines on Climate Risk Management for Scheduled Commercial Banks
policy.description: RBI released binding climate risk management guidelines requiring all SCBs to integrate physical and transition risk into ICAAP by March 2027. Banks with assets over ₹10 lakh crore must submit climate stress test results annually starting FY2027-28.

policy.title: SEBI Expands BRSR Core to Top 500 Listed Companies
policy.description: SEBI mandated BRSR Core reporting with reasonable assurance for top 500 companies by market cap from FY2026-27, adding Scope 3 emissions and water intensity metrics. Value chain disclosure requirements now extend to material suppliers constituting 75% of procurement.

policy.title: IRDAI Releases Exposure Draft on Index-Based Insurance Products
policy.description: IRDAI published draft regulations permitting insurers to launch parametric products for agriculture and SME sectors using satellite and weather indices. Basis risk disclosure and trigger transparency requirements included; comments due by 15 May 2026.

policy.title: Basel Committee Finalizes Pillar 3 Climate Risk Disclosure Framework
policy.description: BCBS issued final Pillar 3 disclosure requirements for climate-related financial risks, effective January 2028 for G-SIBs and January 2029 for other internationally active banks. Includes standardized templates for physical risk exposure by geography and sector.

policy.title: ISSB Releases Guidance on Emerging Market Application of IFRS S2
policy.description: ISSB published implementation guidance for IFRS S2 in emerging markets, including proportionality provisions for SMEs and phased Scope 3 timelines. India referenced as a jurisdiction-specific adoption case with alignment to BRSR framework.

policy.title: EU Commission Clarifies CSRD Value Chain Obligations for Third-Country Suppliers
policy.description: European Commission issued FAQ clarifying that Indian suppliers to EU companies must provide climate and human rights data under CSRD value chain requirements by 2027. Affects estimated 4,000+ Indian exporters in textiles, chemicals, and auto components.

competitors.title: Jupiter Intelligence Partners with HDFC Bank for Physical Risk Analytics
competitors.description: Jupiter announced a multi-year partnership with HDFC Bank to provide asset-level flood and heat stress analytics for the bank's commercial real estate portfolio across 12 Indian metros. Deal reportedly valued at $3.5M over three years.

competitors.title: Sprih Raises $18M Series B for BRSR Automation Platform
competitors.description: Bangalore-based Sprih closed Series B led by Sequoia India and Omnivore, citing 3x ARR growth and 85 enterprise clients including three PSU banks. Funds earmarked for BRSR Core module and supply chain emissions tracking.

competitors.title: XDI Launches Physical Risk API for Emerging Market Banks
competitors.description: XDI released a new API product specifically designed for banks in India, Southeast Asia, and Africa, covering 14 physical hazards at asset level. Pricing starts at $50K annually for up to 100,000 asset assessments.

competitors.title: Moody's Integrates India-Specific Physical Risk Scores into Credit Ratings
competitors.description: Moody's announced integration of physical climate risk scores into credit assessments for Indian corporate issuers, covering flood, cyclone, and heat stress. Methodology paper indicates potential 1-2 notch impact for high-exposure sectors.

competitors.title: Watershed Signs Enterprise Deal with Tata Group for Scope 3 Tracking
competitors.description: San Francisco-based Watershed secured a group-wide contract with Tata Sons covering 15 operating companies for enterprise carbon accounting and supplier engagement. First major India conglomerate deal for the $1.8B-valued unicorn.

competitors.title: CarbonMint Launches Parametric Weather Product for Agri-NBFCs
competitors.description: CarbonMint unveiled a parametric insurance distribution platform for agri-focused NBFCs, using IMD rainfall data as trigger index. Pilot with Samunnati covers 12,000 farmers across Tamil Nadu and Karnataka.

research.title: Physical Climate Risk in Indian Bank Portfolios: A Stress Testing Framework
research.description: RBI's first comprehensive climate stress test methodology finds that a 2°C scenario could increase NPAs by 1.8-3.2% for banks with high agricultural and coastal exposure. Recommends sectoral concentration limits and climate-adjusted provisioning.

research.title: Scaling Parametric Insurance in South Asia: Opportunities and Barriers
research.description: IFC study estimates India's parametric insurance market potential at $2.8B by 2030, with MSME and agriculture segments accounting for 65%. Identifies basis risk transparency and distribution costs as primary adoption barriers.

research.title: ESG Data Gaps in Emerging Markets: Implications for Sustainable Finance
research.description: World Bank analysis of 2,400 EM companies finds only 23% have auditable Scope 1-2 emissions data versus 78% in developed markets. Recommends capacity building for local data providers and regulatory incentives for disclosure.

research.title: Climate Scenarios for Financial Risk Assessment in Asia
research.description: NGFS released Asia-specific climate scenarios with downscaled physical risk projections for India, showing 40% increase in extreme precipitation events and 15-day extension of heat waves by 2050 under current policies scenario.

research.title: Sigma 2026: Natural Catastrophe Losses and the Protection Gap
research.description: Swiss Re's annual sigma report records $156B in global insured nat-cat losses for 2025, with India experiencing $4.2B in economic losses but only $800M insured—an 81% protection gap, highest among major economies.

research.title: Climate Finance in India: Tracking Flows and Measuring Impact
research.description: CPI finds India's tracked climate finance reached $49B in FY2025, up 28% YoY, but adaptation finance remains at only 8% of total. Private sector contribution increased to 54%, driven primarily by renewable energy investments.

ourRead.headline: RBI's binding climate risk framework creates an immediate addressable market—but global players are already moving on Indian banks.
body: This month's signals converge on a single theme: India's climate risk infrastructure for financial institutions is shifting from voluntary exploration to mandatory implementation. The RBI's final guidelines, SEBI's BRSR expansion, and IRDAI's parametric draft collectively create a regulatory trifecta that will force every major Indian FI to operationalize climate risk within 18-24 months. The question is no longer whether banks will need this capability, but who will provide it.

The RBI stress testing framework (policy-1, research-1) is the most consequential development for StepChange. The requirement for annual climate stress test submissions from large banks starting FY2027-28 creates a hard deadline and a clear procurement trigger. Critically, the RBI methodology explicitly calls out physical hazard data at asset level and sector-specific PD/LGD adjustments—precisely StepChange's core capability. The timing window for enterprise sales is now 12-18 months before banks must have systems operational.

Jupiter's HDFC partnership (comp-1) is a strategic warning shot. A global player with superior brand recognition has landed a flagship Indian account, validating market timing but also demonstrating that Indian banks will pay premium prices ($1M+ annually) for credible physical risk analytics. Sprih's Series B (comp-2) shows that Indian ESG software is attracting growth capital, though their BRSR focus is compliance automation rather than analytical depth. StepChange's differentiation must be quantitative rigor—PD/LGD translation that Jupiter and Sprih cannot replicate.

The IFC parametric insurance study (research-2) and CarbonMint's agri-NBFC launch (comp-6) validate our second strategic bet but reveal a sequencing challenge. The $2.8B market opportunity is real, but distribution complexity and basis risk remain barriers. CarbonMint's pilot model—partnering with specialized NBFCs rather than going direct—suggests StepChange should prioritize the data layer and product design capability rather than building distribution. The IRDAI draft (policy-3) requiring trigger transparency creates a regulatory moat for providers with credible hazard data.

One thing to watch: Basel's Pillar 3 finalization (policy-4) will cascade through RBI within 18 months—prepare a regulatory roadmap showing clients how StepChange capabilities map to both RBI and eventual Basel requirements. One action for this week: reach out to HDFC's risk team to understand what Jupiter is delivering and where gaps remain—there is likely room for a complementary Indian provider with deeper local hazard data and regulatory familiarity.
